question_title: Will a red card be shown in the FIFA World Cup 2026 Round of 32 match played on June 28, 2026?
summary: For a single FIFA World Cup 2026 Round of 32 knockout match on June 28, I estimate an 18% probability of at least one red card. This is based on the tension between very low recent World Cup knockout rates (2018/2022: ~6% of matches had a red card) and the historical long-term average (~28%). The 2026 tournament data so far shows only 3 red cards in ~24 matches (all in the opening match), contradicting the question description's claim of ~20. New disciplinary rules exist but haven't produced elevated rates. The knockout stage historically produces ~2x the group stage rate due to elimination pressure. My Monte Carlo simulation, weighted scenario analysis, and base rate weighting all converge around 18-19%.

factors.description: Recent World Cup knockout red card rate is very low — 2018/2022 had only 1 red card each across 16 knockout matches (~6% of matches had ≥1 red card), representing a structural shift in the VAR era

Historical long-term knockout stage rate is significantly higher (~28% of matches had ≥1 red card across 1998-2022, 40 red cards in 112 matches), suggesting the recent low could partly be small-sample variance
New disciplinary rules for 2026 (covering mouth while confronting opponent, captain-only approach to referee, expanded VAR) could increase red card frequency
2026 group stage data so far: only 3 red cards in ~24 Matchday 1 matches (all in one opening match), suggesting new rules haven't dramatically elevated the base rate
Knockout stage elimination pressure, extra time possibility, and tactical fouling historically produce ~2x the group stage red card rate — well-established across decades of data
Question description's claim of ~20 red cards by June 13 is contradicted by actual data showing only 3 — the LLM-generated background overstates the elevated rate narrative

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will a red card be shown in the FIFA World Cup 2026 Round of 32 match on June 28, 2026?

**Probability: 18%**

### Key Findings

1. **The question description is inaccurate.** It claims "roughly 20 red cards" by June 13, but actual data shows only **3 red cards** in the entire 2026 tournament through June 17 — all from the opening match (Mexico vs South Africa). This is an LLM-generated question with hallucinated statistics.

2. **Recent World Cup knockout rates are very low.** Both 2018 and 2022 had just 1 red card across 16 knockout matches each (~6% of matches). This represents the VAR-era structural shift.

3. **Historical long-term rates are higher.** Across 1998-2022, ~28% of knockout matches produced at least one red card (40 total in 112 matches), but the trend has been sharply downward since 2006.

4. **New rules provide modest upward pressure.** Automatic reds for mouth-covering, captain-only approach rules, and expanded VAR exist but haven't materialized in elevated card rates during the 2026 group stage.

5. **Knockout matches are higher intensity.** The ~2x multiplier over group stage rates is well-established historically, giving some upward push from the current tournament baseline.

### Computational Convergence
- Monte Carlo simulation: 18.1%
- Weighted scenario analysis: 19.1%  
- Weighted base rate estimate: 21.2%

All approaches converge around 18-21%, supporting my estimate of **0.18**.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will a red card be shown in the FIFA World Cup 2026 Round of 32 match played on June 28, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44090)
[2011 FIFA Beach Soccer World Cup disciplinary record](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_FIFA_Beach_Soccer_World_Cup_disciplinary_record)

[FIFA World Cup 2026 stats: 3 red cards, Jiménez 47, South Korea's 3 in a row](https://www.espn.com/soccer/story/_/id/49035302/fifa-world-cup-2026-stats-3-red-cards-raul-jimenez-47-korea-republic-3-row)
[Most Red Cards in a Single World Cup Tournament](https://www.fourfourtwo.com/competition/most-red-cards-in-a-single-world-cup-tournament)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if at least one red card is shown to any player, substitute, or substituted player during the FIFA World Cup 2026 Round of 32 match scheduled for 28 June 2026 (Match 73), as recorded on FIFA's official match page for that fixture at https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/scores-fixtures (or the linked individual match center). It resolves NO if no red card is shown in that match. A red card counts whether issued as a direct red or as the result of a second yellow card (two cautions in the same match). If the official FIFA source is unavailable, resolution will rely on [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the match's disciplinary record.
fine_print: Only the single Round of 32 match officially scheduled by FIFA for 28 June 2026 (the calendar date in the venue's local timezone) counts. If FIFA reschedules so that more than one Round of 32 match falls on 28 June 2026 local date, the question resolves YES if a red card is shown in any of those matches. Red cards shown to coaches, team staff, or non-playing personnel do not count — only cards shown to players, substitutes, or substituted players. A card rescinded or overturned by VAR or the referee during the match (such that no red card stands at the final whistle, including any extra time) does not count. If the match is postponed, the question resolves based on that match whenever it is played, provided it is completed before 23:59 UTC on 27 June 2026 [resolution window close interpreted as the question's resolution date]; if the match cannot be completed before the resolution date, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Recent World Cup knockout red card rate is very low (2018/2022: ~6% of matches had ≥1 red card)
Historical long-term knockout stage rate is significantly higher (~28% of matches had ≥1 red card, 1998-2022 average)
New disciplinary rules (mouth-covering, captain-only approach, expanded VAR) could increase red cards
2026 group stage data so far: only 3 red cards in ~24 matches (all in one match), suggesting new rules haven't dramatically elevated rate
Knockout stage intensity: elimination pressure, extra time possibility, tactical fouling historically produces ~2x group stage rate
Question description claims ~20 red cards by June 13, but actual data shows only 3 — LLM-generated question contains inaccurate background

input.anchor: Historical base rate: ~28% of World Cup knockout matches from 1998-2022 had at least one red card (Poisson estimate from 40 red cards in 112 knockout matches). Recent tournaments (2018-2022) show dramatically lower rate of ~6%.
input.assessment: This is a threshold question: will a single knockout match produce at least one red card? The key tension is between the very low recent rates (2018/2022 had ~6% of knockout matches with red cards) and the historical long-term average (~28%). New disciplinary rules exist but haven't produced elevated red card rates in the 2026 tournament so far — only 3 red cards total in ~24 Matchday 1 matches, all concentrated in the opening match. The question description's claim of ~20 red cards by June 13 is factually incorrect based on my research (LLM-generated question with hallucinated statistics). The Monte Carlo simulation and scenario analysis both converge around 18-19%. The knockout stage multiplier (~2x group stage) is well-established historically. My estimate of ~18% represents a compromise between the very low recent baseline (~6%) and the structural factors that push the rate higher (knockout intensity, new rules).
input.calibration_notes: Anchor divergence: My estimate (18%) sits between the long-term average (28%) and the very recent rate (6%), weighted toward the recent trend. The strong downward trend from 2006-2022 is the most informative signal. New rules provide modest upward pressure but haven't materialized in the 2026 data so far. Not hedging toward 50% — the evidence clearly supports a probability well below coin-flip.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. How many more red cards will accumulate in the remaining ~72 group stage matches (Matchdays 2-3) before June 28? If the rate jumps significantly, the knockout estimate should also increase. 2. Whether the new rules produce a delayed uptick as referees become more aggressive with enforcement in later rounds. 3. Which specific teams play in Match 73 — some teams are historically more prone to red cards.
input.tool_audit: Useful: research() sub-agent provided accurate red card counts and historical data. web_search provided confirmation articles. execute_code enabled Monte Carlo and scenario analysis. Failed/empty: ESPN discipline stats page couldn't be fetched (no content extracted). ESPN article page also returned no content. The question description contains inaccurate data (claimed ~20 red cards when actual is 3).
input.update_triggers: - If 10+ more red cards are issued in Matchdays 2-3 (pushing total above 13), I'd move up to ~25%. - If 0-2 more red cards are issued, I'd move down to ~12%. - If one of the teams involved is known for aggressive play (e.g., South Africa based on their 2 red cards in the opening match), I'd adjust upward modestly.
input.process_reflection: The research tools worked well for gathering historical and current data. The code sandbox enabled proper quantitative analysis. The main friction point was the question description containing clearly inaccurate information (claiming ~20 red cards when the actual count is 3), which required verification rather than trust. The ESPN stats page failing to render was a minor issue. The workflow of research → computation → reflection feels appropriate for this question type.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria
Resolution criteria are fully specified and were fetched from the Metaculus question page (post_id 44090). The agent engaged with the specific terms: single Match 73 on June 28, player/substitute/substituted-player only, VAR-overturned cards don't count. ✓

### Hallucinated Evidence
All six factors have trace support:
- **Factor 1** ("~6% of knockout matches had ≥1 red card" for 2018/2022): Confirmed by execute_code output showing 1 red card in 16 knockout matches = 0.062/match, Poisson P(≥1) = 6.1%. ✓
- **Factor 2** ("~28% historical long-term"): Confirmed by code output showing average fraction of KO matches with ≥1 red (1998-2022) = 0.282 (28.2%). ✓
- **Factor 3** (new disciplinary rules): Sourced from question background, FourFourTwo article fetch, and research sub-agent (though the sub-agent relied on training knowledge for rule details). ✓
- **Factor 4** ("only 3 red cards in ~24 matches"): Confirmed by research sub-agent, ESPN article title ("FIFA World Cup 2026 stats: 3 red cards"), FourFourTwo fetch ("Current red card count at 2026 World Cup: 3 red cards"), and Wikipedia group articles. ✓
- **Factor 5** ("~2x group stage rate"): Confirmed by research showing knockout 0.36 vs group 0.18 per match, ratio 1.88. ✓
- **Factor 6** (question description inaccuracy): Correctly identified — multiple sources confirm 3 reds total vs. the question's claim of "roughly 20." ✓

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 and 2 draw on the same historical knockout red card dataset (Wikipedia "List of FIFA World Cup red cards"), just different time windows. They pull in opposite directions (logit -1.2 and +0.5), which is a reasonable analytical decomposition rather than problematic same-direction double-counting. No issue.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions. Factor 6 (logit=-0.3, negative) says the actual red card rate is lower than claimed in the question, pushing probability down. ✓

### Contradictory Assessment
The assessment reaches 18%, while factors sum to -0.66 (implying ~34%). The agent explicitly acknowledges this 16pp gap and justifies it by giving more weight to the 2018-2022 downward trend. This is a transparent judgment call, not a hidden contradiction. The dominant factor (Factor 1, effective logit -0.96) supports the direction of the adjustment. ✓

### Cross-Question Consistency (Binary vs Numeric)
The agent previously forecasted question 44073 ("How many red cards in group stage?") with median 13 in 72 matches, implying a group-stage rate of 0.18/match. With the agent's 2x knockout multiplier, this implies knockout P(≥1) ≈ 30%. The agent's 18% binary estimate is 12pp lower. However, these questions target different quantities (group stage total vs. single knockout match), were answered in different sessions, and the binary question explicitly gives heavier weight to recent (2018-2022) data. The gap is explainable by different modeling choices and is not a direct CDF-threshold inconsistency. Not flagged as a fail.

### Worldview Research Consistency
The worldview entry `2026-fifa-world-cup-group-stage-red-cards-results-8584b591.json` says 2018 had 1 group stage red card, while the main research entry used in this session says ~3. This discrepancy is minor (both represent extremely low rates) and doesn't affect forecast direction.

### Anchor Divergence
The stated anchor has two components: 28% (long-term) and 6% (recent). The 18% estimate is 10pp below the long-term anchor but 12pp above the recent anchor — a compromise position. The departure from 28% is driven by the dominant factor (Factor 1, effective logit -0.96), which is the strongest single factor. This is a reasonable, evidence-driven departure.

### Counterargument Quality
The counterargument is substantive: it identifies the small sample problem with 2018/2022 data (only 32 knockout matches), argues new rules could be a genuine regime change, and identifies team-specific factors. It provides concrete quantitative thresholds that would change the estimate. ✓

### Confidence Assessment
0.45 confidence is appropriate given high historical variance (6% to 53% across tournaments), unknown team composition, and remaining group stage matches that could shift the picture. ✓

### Probability Assessment
The 18% estimate is at the lower end of the agent's own computational models (Monte Carlo: 18.1%, weighted scenarios: 19.1%, weighted base rate: 21.2%). The agent justifies this by weighting recent data more heavily. My independent assessment based on the evidence: 15-20% is reasonable. The recent 2018-2022 data (6% per match) represents a genuine structural shift (VAR era), while historical rates and new rules create upward pressure. The 2026 tournament data (3 reds in 24 matches, all in one game) is consistent with the low-rate regime continuing. I find 18% defensible and consistent with the evidence.

### Tool Audit
The agent claims ESPN pages couldn't be fetched. Ground truth confirms fetch_url had 22.2% error rate (2 errors in 9 calls), consistent with the trace showing two ESPN fetches returning no content. ✓

### Summary
No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, no missing resolution criteria, no problematic double-counting, and no meaningful probability disagreement. The cross-question consistency gap with the group-stage forecast is explainable by different modeling choices. The forecast is well-grounded in historical data and current tournament evidence.
